name: La Nava de Santiago Circular Route
description: La Nava de Santiago — circular is a moderate 8.6-mile (13.9 km) jogging route with 346 feet (105 metres) of elevation gain, taking about 1 h
region_intro: Jogging routes around Merida offer a diverse landscape for runners, characterized by generally flat urban terrain, historic avenues, and accessible green spaces. The region features a mix of well-maintained city parks, tree-lined boulevards, and ecological reserves. Runners can explore paths alongside reservoirs and natural trails, providing varied environments for different running preferences. Best jogging routes around Merida The most popular jogging route is Ride to the Prosperina Reservoir, a 3.5 miles (5.7 km) moderate trail that takes about 36 minutes to complete. This route offers views of the reservoir and surrounding natural areas. Another top favourite among local runners is Route Peñas Blancas by the Minas de la Zaza — circular from La Zarza, a difficult 10.9 miles (17.6 km) path. This circular route features significant elevation changes, traversing varied terrain. Local runners also love the Guadiana Natural Trail, a 10.3 miles (16.5 km) trail leading through natural landscapes, often completed in about 1 hour 51 minutes. Jogging around Merida is defined by urban green spaces, historic boulevards, and natural trails along rivers and reservoirs. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy city runs to more challenging routes with varied elevations. The routes in Merida are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.9 stars from more than 20 reviews. More than 1400 runners have used komoot to explore Merida's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available around Merida?
A: There are over 130 diverse running routes around Merida, catering to various preferences and fitness levels. These include urban paths, historic boulevards, and natural trails along reservoirs and green spaces.

Q: What are some easy running routes suitable for beginners in Merida?
A: For those looking for an easier run, Merida offers several options. The city's generally flat terrain is ideal. Consider exploring the paved pathways of &lt;strong&gt;Parque La Plancha&lt;/strong&gt; or the tree-lined &lt;strong&gt;Paseo de Montejo&lt;/strong&gt;, especially on Sundays when parts are closed to traffic. While specific easy routes from our guide are limited, many urban parks provide gentle paths.

Q: Are there any circular jogging routes in the Merida area?
A: Yes, Merida features several excellent circular running routes. A notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1282202019/route-penas-blancas-by-the-minas-de-la-zaza-circular-from-la-zarza" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route Peñas Blancas by the Minas de la Zaza — circular from La Zarza&lt;/a&gt;, which is a more challenging 10.9-mile path. Another great choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1282211211/run-to-zona-de-nidos-de-cigueenas-rio-guadiana-circular-desde-merida" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Run to Zona de Nidos de Cigüeñas, Río Guadiana — circular desde Mérida&lt;/a&gt;, offering a moderate 9.1-mile loop. For a slightly shorter moderate option, try the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1282185327/ascent-to-the-castillo-de-alange-circular-by-the-cerro-de-la-culebra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ascent to the Castillo de Alange — circular by the Cerro de la Culebra&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the best time of day to go jogging in Merida, considering the climate?
A: Merida's climate is hot and humid year-round. It is highly advisable to run during the early morning or near sunset to avoid the midday heat, especially between April and June when temperatures can reach 40°C. Many locals prefer early morning runs, and facilities like Estadio Salvador Alvarado open early to accommodate this.

Q: Can I find running routes with interesting landmarks or viewpoints in Merida?
A: Absolutely! Merida's routes often combine natural beauty with historical sights. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1282185327/ascent-to-the-castillo-de-alange-circular-by-the-cerro-de-la-culebra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ascent to the Castillo de Alange — circular by the Cerro de la Culebra&lt;/a&gt; offers views of the historic Castillo de Alange. You can also find scenic spots like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5326669" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Views of the Alange Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; along some trails. The &lt;strong&gt;Paseo de Montejo&lt;/strong&gt; itself is lined with beautiful historic mansions, offering a 'runseeing' experience.

Q: Are there any family-friendly running paths in Merida?
A: Yes, Merida offers several family-friendly options. &lt;strong&gt;Parque La Plancha&lt;/strong&gt; is a modern park with paved pathways suitable for running, biking, and skating, along with a lake and extensive green areas, making it popular for families. &lt;strong&gt;Parque Lineal 'Los Paseos'&lt;/strong&gt; and the adjacent &lt;strong&gt;Del Poniente Ecological Park&lt;/strong&gt; also provide tree-lined paths in a natural setting that families can enjoy.

Q: What do other runners say about the jogging trails in Merida?
A: The running routes in Merida are highly rated by the komoot community, with an impressive average score of 4.9 stars from over 20 reviews. More than 1400 runners have used komoot to explore Merida's varied terrain, often praising the mix of urban green spaces, historic boulevards, and natural trails along rivers and reservoirs.

Q: Are there any running routes that pass by reservoirs or natural monuments?
A: Many routes in the Merida area feature beautiful reservoirs and natural monuments. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992989932/ride-to-the-prosperina-reservoir" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ride to the Prosperina Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; offers direct views of the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6309612" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Proserpina Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;. Another great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992978510/cornalvo-reservoir-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cornalvo Reservoir Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you past the stunning &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3071953" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cornalvo Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;. You can also explore routes near natural monuments like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4360372" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Rugidero&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What are some longer running routes for experienced runners in Merida?
A: For experienced runners seeking a challenge, Merida offers several longer and more difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1280676889/guadiana-natural-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guadiana Natural Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a 10.3-mile path through natural landscapes. Another demanding option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1280289157/el-rugidero-y-la-presa-de-cornalvo-circular-desde-la-presa-de-cornalvo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Rugidero y la Presa de Cornalvo — circular desde la Presa de Cornalvo&lt;/a&gt;, an 11.4-mile difficult route. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1282179782/torremejia-and-via-de-la-plata-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Torremejía and Vía de la Plata Loop&lt;/a&gt; also provides a substantial 9.3-mile run.

Q: Are there any running routes near Merida that offer a unique natural experience, like cenotes?
A: While the running routes themselves may not directly pass through cenotes, the Yucatán Peninsula is famous for these natural sinkholes. The natural settings around cenotes, such as those in the Santa Bárbara cenotes ecological park, offer a unique landscape to visit. You could plan a run in a nearby area and then cool off with a swim in a cenote as part of a day trip, combining your run with a distinct natural experience.

Q: Are there running routes that are suitable for dogs in Merida?
A: Many of Merida's outdoor spaces, especially the ecological parks and natural trails, are generally dog-friendly. &lt;strong&gt;Parque Lineal 'Los Paseos'&lt;/strong&gt; and &lt;strong&gt;Del Poniente Ecological Park&lt;/strong&gt; offer ample green space and paths where dogs are often welcome, provided they are on a leash. Always ensure to check local regulations for specific parks and trails regarding pets.

Q: Where can I find running routes with good parking facilities in Merida?
A: Many of Merida's larger parks and trailheads offer convenient parking. For urban runs, areas around &lt;strong&gt;Paseo de Montejo&lt;/strong&gt; and &lt;strong&gt;Parque La Plancha&lt;/strong&gt; typically have parking available, though it might be paid or street parking. For routes further afield, such as those near reservoirs like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e992978510/cornalvo-reservoir-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cornalvo Reservoir Trail&lt;/a&gt;, designated parking areas are usually provided at the starting points.

faqs.question: How long does it take to complete the La Nava de Santiago — circular trail?
faqs.answer: This jogging route is approximately 13.9 km long and typically takes about 1 hour and 40 minutes to complete. This duration is an estimate for jogging and may vary based on your pace and any stops you make.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this jogging route?
faqs.answer: The La Nava de Santiago — circular trail is rated as moderate. With an elevation gain of around 105 meters, it offers a good challenge for joggers without being overly strenuous.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the La Nava de Santiago — circular trail?
faqs.answer: While specific details for this exact route are limited, similar circular trails in the region often feature a mix of easily traversable tracks, paths, and potentially some rural road sections. You can expect varied surfaces, possibly including dirt, gravel, and some paved areas, suitable for jogging.

faqs.question: Is the La Nava de Santiago — circular trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Generally, rural trails in this region are dog-friendly. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through agricultural areas or near livestock. Be sure to carry water for your pet.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this route?
faqs.answer: Spring and autumn are typically the best seasons for jogging in this area, offering milder temperatures and pleasant scenery. In late spring, you might even encounter abundant wildflowers. Summers can be hot, so early morning or late afternoon jogs are advisable, while winters can be cooler but still enjoyable on clear days.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the La Nava de Santiago — circular trail?
faqs.answer: As the trail is circular and starts and ends in the settlement of La Nava de Santiago, you should be able to find parking within or on the outskirts of the village. Look for designated public parking areas or safe roadside spots.

faqs.question: Are there any points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The route itself is centered around the settlement of La Nava de Santiago. While specific natural or historical highlights directly on this particular jogging path are not detailed, the charm of rural villages, local landscapes, and potentially historical elements are common in similar regional trails.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, permits or entrance fees are generally not required for accessing rural jogging and hiking trails in this region, including the La Nava de Santiago — circular route. It is freely accessible to the public.

faqs.question: Is the trail suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty and 13.9 km distance, this trail is best suited for joggers with some experience. Beginners might find the distance challenging, but the relatively low elevation gain makes it manageable for those looking to push their limits.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a jog on this trail?
faqs.answer: For this moderate jogging route, bring appropriate running shoes, comfortable athletic clothing, and sufficient water, especially on warmer days. A small backpack for essentials, a phone for navigation or emergencies, and sun protection are also recommended.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like cafes or restrooms near the trail?
faqs.answer: Since the trail starts and ends in the settlement of La Nava de Santiago, you may find local amenities such as cafes, bars, or small shops within the village itself. It's advisable to check opening hours, especially in smaller rural communities.
